title: SEQUENTIAL COVARIATE SHIFT DETECTION USING CLASSIFIER TWO-SAMPLE TESTS [PDF]

paragraphs.text:
Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

## SEQUENTIAL COVARIATE SHIFT DETECTION USING CLASSIFIER TWO-SAMPLE TESTS

## **Anonymous authors**

Paper under double-blind review

## ABSTRACT

A standard assumption in supervised learning is that the training data and test data are from the same distribution. However, this assumption often fails to hold in practice, which can cause the learned model to perform poorly. We consider the problem of detecting _covariate shift_ , where the covariate distribution shifts but the conditional distribution of labels given covariates remains the same. This problem can naturally be solved using a two-sample test— _i.e._ , test whether the current test distribution of covariates equals the training distribution of covariates. Our algorithm builds on _classifier tests_ , which train a discriminator to distinguish train and test covariates, and then use the accuracy of this discriminator as a test statistic. A key challenge is that classifier tests assume given a fixed set of test covariates. In practice, test covariates often arrive sequentially over time—e.g., a self-driving car observes a stream of images while driving. Furthermore, covariate shift can occur multiple times— _i.e._ , shift and then shift back later or gradually shift over time. To address these challenges, our algorithm trains the discriminator online. Furthermore, it evaluates test accuracy using each new covariate before taking a gradient step; this strategy avoids constructing a held-out test set, which can reduce sample efficiency. We prove that this optimization preserves the correctness— _i.e._ , our algorithm achieves a desired bound on the false positive rate. In our experiments, we show that our algorithm efficiently detects covariate shifts on ImageNet.

## 1 INTRODUCTION

A key challenge facing deep neural networks is their sensitivity to changes in the data distribution. In particular, supervised learning traditionally assumes that the training and test data are from the same distribution (Vapnik, 1998), but this assumption often fails in practice. For example, an autonomous car using perception to identify obstacles needs to be robust to shifts such as changes in the weather and lighting conditions. We focus on _covariate shift_ (Shimodaira, 2000), where there is a shift in the covariate distribution _p_ ( _x_ ), and the conditional label distribution _p_ ( _y | x_ ) remains unchanged. Covariate shift can reduce model performance (Sugiyama & M¨uller, 2005), invalidate uncertainty estimates (Ovadia et al., 2019; Park et al., 2020), and affect model selection (Sugiyama et al., 2007).

One strategy is to devise an algorithm to detect covariate shift; if detected, the algorithm can alert the user that predictions may be unreliable. Covariate shift detection can be formulated as two-sample hypothesis test (Gretton et al., 2012a; Rabanser et al., 2018; Liu et al., 2020), where the goal is to determine whether two sets of examples are from the same distribution. To test for covariate shift, we choose the first sample to be the data used to train the model and the second sample to be recent test data given as input to the model. Then, the detector returns “covariate shift” if the hypothesis test indicates that the two samples are from different distributions and “no shift” otherwise.

We propose a detection algorithm based on classifier tests (Lopez-Paz & Oquab, 2017; Cheng & Cloninger, 2019; Kim et al., 2021), which use the accuracy of a classifier trained to distinguish the two samples as the test statistic. In particular, if the two samples are from the same distribution, then the accuracy should be 1 _/_ 2; otherwise, it should be _>_ 1 _/_ 2. Since the test statistic follows a binomial distribution, we use the Clopper-Pearson interval (Clopper & Pearson, 1934) (an exact confidence interval for the unknown success probability of the Binomial distribution) to derive the cutoff. In contrast, prior work relies on asymptotics to derive the cutoff, which results in approximations.

1

Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

A key challenge is that the test examples are typically obtained over time—e.g., an autonomous robot continuously perceives its environment, and we want to detect if its distribution of observations shifts at any time. There are two key challenges to leveraging classifier tests in this setting. First, they rely on training a classifier to distinguish training and test examples; doing so on every step would be computationally intractable. Second, they rely on a held-out test set to estimate the test statistic, but constructing such a set online would reduce sample efficiency.

Rather than train a classifier at each step, our proposed algorithm trains a model online using stochastic gradient descent. Then, rather than construct a held-out test set, our algorithm evaluates the accuracy of the model online using each example before taking a gradient step on that example. We prove that this strategy results in an unbiased estimate of the model accuracy; as a consequence, the finite-sample guarantees on the false positive rate provided by the sequential test continue to hold. In addition, we prove bounds on the false negative rate under mild conditions on the classifier (i.e., it achieves nontrivial accuracy distinguishing the two distributions).

We evaluate our approach on both synthetic and natural shifts on the ImageNet (Russakovsky et al., 2015) dataset. In particular, we demonstrate that our approach achieves better sample efficiency than baseline algorithms; furthermore, it uniformly satisfies the desired false positive rate. Thus, our algorithm is an effective strategy for sequential covariate shift detection.

**Contributions.** We formulate (sequential) covariate shift detection as a two-sample test, and propose a novel algorithm to solve this problem (Section 3). Then, we prove finite sample bounds on false positive rate and false negative rate achieved by our algorithm (Section 4). Finally, we empirically demonstrate that our algorithm effectively detects shifts on ImageNet (Section 5).

**Sequential detection vs. sequential tests.** While we consider the sequential setting, we deliberately choose not use a sequential hypothesis test, since the covariate shift may occur after a delay or gradually over time. A sequential test only applies if _all_ of the test data is shifted. Furthermore, since we are not using sequential tests, the false positive rate bound only holds per-step, not uniformly across all steps. This is necessary: we cannot guarantee that we detect a covariate shift occurring at a later point in time if we constrain the false positive to be bounded uniformly across all steps. In our experiments, we show that the rate of false alarms remains manageable while enabling our algorithm to detect covariate shift in a number of interesting scenarios.

## 2 RELATED WORK

**Covariate shift.** There has been work on training models in the presence of covariate shift. In particular, in the unsupervised domain adaptation setting (Ben-David et al., 2007; Bickel et al., 2007; Ganin et al., 2016), the algorithm has access to labeled examples from the source domain but only unlabeled examples from the target domain, and the goal is to train a model that achieves good performance on the target domain. One strategy is to use importance weighting to upweight source examples that are more similar to target examples (Bickel et al., 2007). Another strategy is to first learn an invariant representation (Ganin et al., 2016), which is an embedding space where the source and target examples are similar, and then train a model on this embedding space using the source examples. If we detect covariate shift, one solution is to retrain the model using these techniques.

**Two-sample tests.** We focus on _classifier two-sample tests (C2ST)_ . In this approach, the idea is to train a binary classifier to distinguish source and target samples, compute a real-valued score based on this classifier as the test statistic, and then use a univariate two-sample test to determine the cutoff for rejecting the null hypothesis (Friedman, 2004). A natural test statistic is the classifier’s accuracy on a held-out test set (Kim et al., 2021; Lopez-Paz & Oquab, 2017), or the differences in the classifier’s logits (Cheng & Cloninger, 2019); in this work, we use the former. One way to compute the cutoff is to use the asymptotic distribution of the test statistic (Lopez-Paz & Oquab, 2017). Nonparametric tests such as permutation tests can also be used (Kim et al., 2021).

Another kind of two-sample test is called a _kernel two-sample test_ . In this approach, the idea is to use the maximum mean discrepancy (MMD) between the two samples according to a given kernel embedding as the test statistic (Gretton et al., 2012a; Chwialkowski et al., 2015; Jitkrittum et al., 2016). The key design decision is the choice of kernel. One strategy is to use a nonparametric kernel such as Gaussian radial basis functions (Gretton et al., 2012a); alternatively, the kernel can also be optimized to minimize the false negative rate of the resulting test (Gretton et al., 2012b). In addition,

2

Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

recent work has shown how to first learn a kernel function in the form of a deep neural network, and then evaluate the MMD distance on a held-out test set (Liu et al., 2020). The test statistic can be chosen based on finite sample bounds or based on its asymptotic distribution (Gretton et al., 2012a). Nonparametric permutation tests can also be used (Liu et al., 2020) with this approach.

**Concept Drift.** In the context of _concept drift_ (Gama et al., 2014), there has been work detecting shifts in _p_ ( _x, y_ ) (Gonc¸alves Jr et al., 2014). However, this work assumes that ground truth labels are provided for test examples, whereas our approach only requires unlabeled test examples. The former is substantially easier, since it suffices to check for drift in the distribution of prediction errors, which is usually very simple (e.g., a Bernoulli distribution for the 0-1 loss), making it easy to test for drift. In contrast, our approach checks for drift in high-dimensional covariates distribution.

**Sequential hypothesis testing.** A closely related problem is sequential hypothesis testing, which adaptively decides whether to reject the null hypothesis as samples become available (Wald, 1945). These approaches can also applied to two-sample testing (Balsubramani & Ramdas, 2015; Lh´eritier & Cazals, 2018; 2019; Manole & Ramdas, 2021). However, as discussed above, they assume that the each distribution of the two samples does not change over time. In contrast, we are interested in the setting where the test examples might initially be from the same distribution as the training examples, but then shift at a later point in time. Sequential tests are not applicable to this setting.

## 3 SEQUENTIAL COVARIATE SHIFT DETECTION

## 3.1 PROBLEM FORMULATION

Let _X_ be the covariate space, _S_ be the source distribution over _X_ , and _Tt_ 1: _t_ 2 = ( _Tt_ 1 _, Tt_ 1+1 _, . . . , Tt_ 2) be a sequence of target distributions over _X_ from time steps _t_ 1 to _t_ 2. On time step _t_ , we consider samples _xt ∼S_ and _x[′] t[∼T][t]_[;][in][practice,] _[S]_[can][be][taken][to][be][the][uniform][distribution][over] the training set. We let _Sw,t_ = ( _xt−w_ +1 _, xt−w_ +2 _, . . . , xt_ ) and _Tw,t_ = ( _x[′] t−w_ +1 _[, x] t[′] −w_ +2 _[, . . . , x] t[′]_[)] denote the recent examples in a time window of a given size _w ∈_ N. Our goal is to detect covariate shift at any step _t_ . More precisely, we want to determine whether _S_ = _T_[¯] _w,t_ , where _T_[¯] _w,t_ =[�] _[t] k_ = _t−w_ +1 _[T][k][/w]_[,] _[i.e.]_[,][whether][the][average][target][distributions][over][the] previous _w_ steps is shifted compared to _S_ . For a fixed step _t_ , this problem is a two-sample hypothesis test (Lehmann & Romano, 2006), where the null hypothesis is _H_ 0 : _S_ = _T_[¯] _w,t_ , and the alternative hypothesis is _H_ 1 : _S_ = _T_[¯] _w,t_ . That is, a two-sample test _f_[ˆ] is designed to compute

Our goal is to design a two-sample test _f_[ˆ] for detecting covariate shift with this data stream. While we can in principle use any two-sample test, our goal is to design one that is both sample and computationally efficient while achieving high accuracy for high-dimensional data such as images. In addition, we want the test _f_[ˆ] to come with finite sample guarantees on the false positive rate. In particular, given _α ∈_ R _>_ 0, if _S_ = _T_[¯] _w,t_ , we want to ensure

Ideally, we also want to provide finite sample bounds on the false negative rate; however, for classifier tests, we can only do so under additional assumptions about the model family used to try and distinguish _S_ and _T_[¯] _w,t_ . Intuitively, we assume that (i) the model family has bounded complexity (e.g., Rademacher complexity), and (ii) some model exists in the family that achieves nontrivial accuracy at distinguishing _S_ and _T_[¯] _w,t_ . Then, our goal is to ensure that if _S_ = _T_[¯] _w,t_ , we have

for some function _M_ ( _α, w_ ) that depends on the model family.

Next, we describe our two-sample test. We build on classifier two-sample test (C2ST) (Lopez-Paz & Oquab, 2017; Kim et al., 2021). The idea is to train a classifier _g_ ˆ _t_ to try and distinguish _Sw,t_

Intuitively, if _S_ and _T_[¯] _w,t_ are different distributions, then _g_ ˆ _t_ should achieve nontrivial accuracy at distinguishing _Sw,t_ from _Tw,t_ (assuming the model family is sufficiently expressive). Alternatively, if _S_ = _T_[¯] _w,t_ , then ˆ _gt_ necessarily achieves a trivial expected accuracy of 1 _/_ 2.

In particular, the accuracy of _g_ ˆ _t_ can be used as a test statistic for the two-sample test. To choose the cutoff for rejecting the null hypothesis, we use the Clopper-Pearson (CP) interval (Clopper & Pearson, 1934) to construct an interval that contains the true accuracy ˆ _gt_ with high probability based on the accuracy of _g_ ˆ _t_ on a test set. More precisely, the CP interval is an exact confidence interval around the empirical estimate of the mean of a Bernoulli random variable. where the probability is taken over _s_ , _α_ is a given confidence level, and ΘCP is a function of the ˆ Binomial random variable _s_ = _n · µ_ ( _z_ 1: _n_ ). The CP interval is concretely defined by

Our algorithm uses the CP interval to determine whether the accuracy of _g_ ˆ _t_ is nontrivial, _i.e._ , _>_ 1 _/_ 2. In particular, the accuracy of _g_ ˆ _t_ is the mean of the Bernoulli random variable 1 (ˆ _gt_ ( _x_ ) = _y_ ), where _y_ is the ground truth indicating whether _x_ is from _S_ or _T_[¯] _w,t_ . Then, our algorithm rejects if the CP interval does not contain 1 _/_ 2, since this condition implies that the accuracy of ˆ _gt_ does not equal 1 _/_ 2 with high probability. We describe this step in detail below.

The key challenge is what data to use as the test dataset to estimate the accuracy of ˆ _gt_ . The traditional strategy is to split the available data into two parts: one to train _g_ ˆ _t_ and a second held-out test set to estimate its accuracy (Lopez-Paz & Oquab, 2017; Kim et al., 2021). However, this approach reduces sample efficiency, which is problematic in our setting since we often want to _w_ to be small.

To address this challenge, our algorithm exploits the conditional independence structure of classifier predictions. In particular, as described below, our algorithm uses each example _xt_ to evaluate the ˆ ˆ accuracy of _gt before_ using it to train _gt_ . In the next section, we prove that this strategy maintains ˆ the independence of our estimate of the accuracy of _gt_ (Lemma 1), and that as a consequence, our algorithm satisfies the desired false positive rate (for a single step _t_ ).

## 3.3 ALGORITHM DETAILS

**Sequential detection algorithm.** At each time step _t_ , we observe a source sample _xt ∼S_ and a target sample _x[′] t[∼T][t]_[.][By][using][these][current][samples][and][previous][samples,][we][detect][covariate] shifts by updating the source-target classifier in online learning. In particular, our algorithm consists of three steps: (1) source-target prediction, (2) covariate shift detection, and (3) online source-target classifier update. The following and Algorithm 1 include details.

## 4 THEORETICAL GUARANTEES

In this section, we describe our finite sample bounds on the false positive and false negative rates of our covariate shift detector _f_[ˆ] ; the key to have valid bounds is proving the independence on predictions _y_ ˆ1 _, . . . ,_ ˆ _yt_ (and _y_ ˆ1 _[′][, . . . ,]_[ ˆ] _[y] t[′]_[) to have a valid Clopper-Pearson interval, since they are seemingly] dependent through the online learned classifier _g_ ˆ _t_ . First, our key result shows that our estimate of the accuracy of _g_ ˆ _t_ is valid— _i.e._ , the predictions _y_ ˆ _i, . . . ,_ ˆ _yj_ are conditionally independent (see Appendix A.1 for a proof), thus the accuracy is the parameter of the Binomial distribution: **Lemma 1.** _If xi, . . . , xj are independent for any i, j ∈_ N _where i < j, y_ ˆ _i, . . . ,_ ˆ _yj are conditionally independent given_ ˆ _gi, . . . ,_ ˆ _gj−_ 1 _._

Our next result says that our algorithm ensures the desired bound _α_ on the false positive rate (i.e., _f_[ˆ] says “covariate shift” when there is no covariate shift). To this end, we exploit the following observation that _any_ source-target classifier makes the expected accuracy of 1 _/_ 2 if there is no covariate shift. Intuitively, if _S_ = _T_[¯] _w,t_ , source-target classification is impossible (Lopez-Paz & Oquab, 2017; Liu et al., 2020); we include this lemma for completeness (see Appendix A.2 for a proof):
**Lemma 2.** _If S_ = _T_[¯] _w,t, we have µ[∗] w,t_[= 1] _[/]_[2] _[ for any source-target classifier]_[ ˆ] _[g][t][.]_

Since the expected accuracy of _g_ ˆ _t_ is 1 _/_ 2 _regardless of_ how we design and learn _g_ ˆ _t_ , and how many samples are used to learn ˆ _gt_ , the Clopper-Pearson interval includes the true accuracy with high probability; thus the false positive rate of the proposed covariate shift detector _f_[ˆ] is effectively controlled by the confidence level of the Clopper-Pearson interval, as follows (see Appendix A.3 for a proof):

Our next result provides a bound on the false negative rate; we first observe that the Clopper-Pearson interval is included in the interval by the Hoeffding’s bound. Intuitively, the Clopper-Pearson interval represents a lower and upper bound of the expected accuracy given an empirical accuracy tailored to a Bernoulli random variable; the Hoeffding’s bound can similarly bound the mean but in a more general setup. Thus, the Clopper-Pearson interval can be smaller (see Appendix A.4 for a proof). **Lemma 4.** _Let s ∼ Binomial_ ( _n, p_ ) _and F_ ( _s_ ; _n, p_ ) _is the CDF of Binomial_ ( _n, p_ ) _; we have_

## 5 EXPERIMENTS

We evaluate the effectiveness of our algorithm at detecting both natural and synthetic covariate shifts of varying forms (e.g., gradual shifts and multiple shifts back and forth), showing that it significantly outperforms natural baselines.

## 5.1 EXPERIMENT SETUP

**Baselines.** We compare our algorithm to three baselines: two of them differ in the way they use the samples at each time step, the third uses Wald’s sequential likelihood test (Wald, 1945). For the first two baselines, while our approach uses all samples to construct the CP interval around the accuracy of the source-target classifier ˆ _gt_ as well as to train ˆ _gt_ , the baseline instead constructs a held-out test set using every _H[th]_ sample. Then, only this held-out test set is used to compute the CP interval, and only the remaining samples are used to train ˆ _gt_ . In our experiments, we used values of _H ∈{_ 2 _,_ 5 _}_ , denoted H2, H5, respectively. For Wald’s test, we consider the Bernoulli random variable with a probability _p_ indicating whether the prediction of the source-target classifier is correct for the given

sample. The hypothesis test is _H_ 0 : _p_ = 0 _._ 5 vs. _H_ 1 : _p_ = 0 _._ 5+ _ϵ_ , where _ϵ_ = 0 _._ 2 in our experiments; we restart the test each time it makes a decision.

This baseline is essentially the online version of an existing classifier two-sample test (C2ST) (Kim et al., 2021; Lopez-Paz & Oquab, 2017), which splits the (fixed) training dataset into a training set to train _g_ ˆ _t_ and a held-out test set to estimate the accuracy of _g_ ˆ _t_ ; thus, _H_ controls the tradeoff between the number of examples in the training set and held-out test set.

**Source-target classifier.** We use a fully-connected neural network with a single hidden layer (with ˆ 128 hidden units) and with the ReLU activation functions as the source-target classifier _gt_ . We use a binary cross-entropy loss for training in conjunction with an SGD optimizer with a learning rate of 0.01 (for natural shift experiments) and 0.001 (for synthetic shift experiments). Finally, since the inputs are ImageNet images (Russakovsky et al., 2015), we use a 2048-dimensional feature vector generated by first running a pretrained ResNet152 model (He et al., 2016) on the images, and then using these features vectors for the covariates of _Sw,t_ and _Tw,t_ .

**Scenarios.** We run each algorithm to test whether the target distribution in the given window is shifted with three different scenarios: multiple shift (“M-shift”), gradually increasing shift (“GIshift”), and gradually increasing-then-decreasing shift (“GID-shift”). Table 1 describes each scenario. For example, the multiple shift scenario proceeds as follows: (i) it starts with no covariate shift at the beginning; (ii) after observing 25% target samples ( _i.e._ , 250 _[th]_ samples for natural shift experiments and 2500 _[th]_ samples for synthetic shift experiments), covariate shift is applied to all target samples (with probability 1) by adding random perturbations for synthetic shift and by drawing samples from a target distribution for natural shift; (iii) after 50% of target samples, it reverts to no covariate shift; and (iv) finally after observing 75% target samples, the covariate shift is applied to the all target samples. Gradually increasing shift and gradually increasing-then-decreasing shift scenarios start with no covariate shift for the first 20% of target samples; then, covariate shift is applied with some probability 0 _< p <_ 1 by gradually changing _p_ over time.

**Stream data generation.** For each shift ( _i.e._ , natural shift and synthetic shift), we have a source dataset _S_ and target datasets _Tt_ , from which we randomly draw source and target samples for each time step _t_ . In particular, we consider a batch of samples for computational efficiency, where we denote the batch size by _B_ ; we use _B_ = 10 for our experiments. That is, we wait for _B_ samples to be collected from the target distribution before checking for covariate shift and the updating the sourcetarget classifier; then, we begin collecting the next batch. Finally, we evaluate each approach using multiple random repetitions, which we denote by _R_ (the value of _R_ depends on each experiment).

## 5.2 NATURAL SHIFT

**Dataset.** First, we consider a natural shift on ImageNet. To construct such a shift, we consider the subset of dog classes; in particular, 120 of the 1000 of the ImageNet classes are of dogs (Khosla et al., 2011). Then, we randomly select half ( _i.e._ , 60) of these classes to be the source dataset, and the other half to be the target dataset; thus, the number of source and target images is 2997 each (after removing duplicated images). As a consequence, the source and target datasets correspond to different dog breeds, which is a kind of natural distribution shift.

**Results.** Figure 1 and Table 2 show results for the natural shift experiment with _w_ = 10 and _α_ = 1%. Figure 1 illustrates detection rates of the four algorithms with _R_ = 100 repetitions (i.e., the fraction of repetitions that reported “covariate shift” at each step). Table 2a shows the number

7

Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

**----- Start of picture text -----**

(a) M-shift (b) GI-shift (c) GID-shift
**----- End of picture text -----**
Figure 1: Detection rate for natural shift with _R_ = 100, _w_ = 10, _α_ = 1%. The black dashed line indicates shifted sample ratio, _i.e._ , the degree (or probability) of covariate shift.
Table 2: Natural shift results with (a) _w_ = 10, _α_ = 1%, and _R_ = 100, and (b) _R_ = 20000 . In (a), we bold the best algorithm. In (b), we bold values that exceed the desired _α_ = 1%.

of shifted samples required to reach at least 80% of covariate shift detection rate under the shift. Table 2b shows false positive rate (FPR) after 50, 100, 150, and 200 samples with _R_ = 20000 repetitions.

**Discussion.** Figure 1 shows the detection rate of each algorithm as each scenario progresses. In multiple shift (Figure 1a) and gradually increasing-then-decreasing shift (Figure 1c), covariate shift disappears after a certain point, and all algorithms correctly detect this change. However, as shown in Table 2a, our approach always requires fewer samples to detect the shift. While H5 does not achieve 80% detection and H2 and Wald reach 80% only for multiple shift change scenario, our approach always detects covariate shift at a rate higher than 80%. Furthermore, for multiple shift, our algorithm requires fewer than half the number of samples compared to H2. In summary, our algorithm is significantly more sample efficient at detecting covariate shift compared to the baselines, most likely since it utilizes all samples for both training the source-target classifier and constructing the CP interval. For FPR, all algorithms always satisfy the FPR bound ( _i.e._ , FPR _≤ α_ ).

## 5.3 SYNTHETIC SHIFT

**Dataset.** Next, we consider a synthetic shift on ImageNet. In particular, we split the original ImageNet validation set into equal sized source and target datasets. To construct the target dataset, we add synthetic perturbations on original images. We (separately) consider five perturbation types from (Hendrycks & Dietterich, 2019)—in particular, Contrast, Defocus Blur, Elastic Transform, Gaussian Blur, and Gaussian Noise, with five different severity levels.

**Results.** The experiment results are shown in Figure 2 and Table 3 for the experiments with the perturbation severity of 2, window size _w_ = 10, and significance level _α_ = 1%. Table 3a shows the number of target samples required by each algorithm to detect the first covariate shift in the detection rate of at least 80%. Table 3b shows the false positive rate (FPR) after 500, 1000, 1500 and 2000 samples for each of the three scenarios. Figure 2 shows the detection rates over multiple repetitions

Figure 2: Detection rate for synthetic shifts with Gaussian noise perturbation, Severity = 2, _R_ = 100, _w_ = 10, _α_ = 1%. The black dashed line indicates shifted sample ratio, _i.e._ , the degree (or probability) of covariate shift. Table 3: Synthetic shift results with (a) Severity = 2, _w_ = 10, _α_ = 1%, and _R_ = 100, and (b) _R_ = 20000. In (a), we bold the best algorithm. In (b), we bold values that exceed the desired _α_ = 1%.
for each of the three scenarios using the Gaussian noise perturbation. Results for other perturbation types and severities are shown in Appendix D.

**Discussion.** As can be seen, our approach outperforms the baselines in terms of sample efficiency for the covariate shift detection as was the case of the natural shift. Our algorithm requires about half as many samples before detecting covariate shift compared to the baselines. In terms of FPR, our approach always satisfies the FPR bound. Finally, Figure 2 shows the accuracy drop with the shifted samples. In particular, the red dotted line shows the accuracy of ResNet152 on the examples in the source and target samples of the given window; as can be seen, the accuracy decreases as the degree of the shift increases. Covariate shift detection can be successfully used to notify a user that an accuracy drop may have occurred.

## 6 CONCLUSION

We have proposed a novel covariate shift detection algorithm, which uses a classifier two-sample test to check whether the current test examples differ in distribution compared to the training examples. Our approach ensures sample efficiency by avoiding the need to split the dataset into a training set and a held-out test set, and instead using all the data to both train the source-target discriminator and to evaluate its accuracy. We prove that even with this optimization, our approach provides finite sample guarantees on the false positive rate at a desired level; we also prove bounds on the false negative rate under a mild conditions on the trained classifier. Finally, we empirically demonstrate that our proposed algorithm is significantly more sample efficient compared to a natural baseline that uses a held-out test set in terms of detecting both natural and synthetic shifts on ImageNet.

**Reproducibility Statement.** For our empirical results, we stated our algorithm in Algorithm 1, hyperparameters in Section 5.1, and dataset setups in Section 5.2 and 5.3. We have included the source code in the supplement for reproducing the experimental results. For our theory, we have included all proofs in Appendix A.

zero_shot__gemini-3-flash-preview.label: Zero Shot (gemini-3-flash-preview)
zero_shot__gemini-3-flash-preview.overall_feedback: The paper presents a clever and practically-motivated adaptation of classifier two-sample tests (C2ST) for sequential covariate shift detection. By using a 'predict-then-update' online strategy, the authors bypass the need for data splitting, which is a significant practical advantage in streaming settings. The theoretical framework leveraging the Clopper-Pearson interval provides strong finite-sample guarantees for the false positive rate. However, there are some technical ambiguities regarding the windowing mechanism and the independence assumptions in Lemma 1 that could be clarified to ensure full reproducibility and theoretical robustness.

comments.title: Ambiguity in Windowing and Sample Independence
comments.quote: Let $Sw,t = (xt-w+1, xt-w+2, . . . , xt)$ and $Tw,t = (x'_{t-w+1}, x'_{t-w+2}, . . . , x'_t)$ denote the recent examples in a time window of a given size $w \in \mathbb{N}$. ... our algorithm returns 'covariate shift' if $1/2 \notin \Theta_{CP}(2w\hat{\mu}_{w,t}, 2w; \alpha)$
comments.explanation: The text suggests that at each step $t$, the test is performed using a sliding window of size $w$. If the test is conducted at every $t$, consecutive test statistics $\hat{\mu}_{w,t}$ and $\hat{\mu}_{w,t+1}$ will share $w-1$ samples, creating significant temporal correlation. While Theorem 3 provides a per-step guarantee (as acknowledged in Section 1), the use of the Clopper-Pearson interval (designed for i.i.d. Binomial trials) on a sliding window requires careful handling. I initially thought the authors meant disjoint windows, but the text says 'at each time step t'. If the windows overlap, the 'independence' claim in Lemma 1 for the sequence of predictions needs to clearly distinguish between the independence of the Bernoulli trials *within* a single test at time $t$ versus the independence of the detector's decisions *across* time.

comments.title: Nuance in Lemma 1 Independence Claim
comments.quote: If $x_i, . . . , x_j$ are independent for any $i, j \in \mathbb{N}$ where $i < j, \hat{y}_i, . . . , \hat{y}_j$ are conditionally independent given $\hat{g}_i, . . . , \hat{g}_{j-1}$.
comments.explanation: The lemma claims conditional independence given the sequence of classifiers. In an online setting where $\hat{g}_t$ is updated using $x_t$, the classifier $\hat{g}_t$ is a function of all previous data $(x_1, ..., x_{t-1})$. While the prediction $\hat{y}_t = \hat{g}_t(x_t)$ is independent of $x_t$ given $\hat{g}_t$, the accuracy estimate $\hat{\mu}_{w,t}$ sums terms where the 'model' used for $\hat{y}_i$ is different for each $i$ in the window. The Binomial distribution assumes a fixed success probability $p$. Here, $p_i$ (the probability of correct classification) changes as the model $\hat{g}_i$ learns. The authors effectively show $\mathbb{E}[\hat{y}_i] = 1/2$ under $H_0$, but since the $p_i$ values are not identical (even if they all equal $1/2$ in expectation), the sum is technically a Poisson Binomial distribution. In the specific case of $H_0$ where $p_i = 1/2$ exactly for all $i$, this collapses to a standard Binomial, justifying the CP interval. However, this logic is non-trivial and the prose glosses over the 'identical' part of 'i.i.d.'

comments.title: Parameter Inconsistency in False Negative Bound
comments.quote: If $S = Tw,t$ and $w - 1 - \lfloor \sqrt{w \log(2/\alpha)} \rfloor \geq 0$, then we have...
comments.explanation: The condition $S = T_{w,t}$ in the theorem statement for the False Negative Rate (Theorem 5) is likely a typo. The context of a False Negative Rate (Type II error) requires the alternative hypothesis $H_1$ to be true, meaning $S \neq T_{w,t}$. Additionally, the quote mentions $S = T_{w,t}$ twice (once in Lemma 2 and once in Theorem 5), but in Theorem 5 it should denote the presence of shift to evaluate the power of the test. Referring to $H_1$ or $S \neq \bar{T}_{w,t}$ would be consistent with standard hypothesis testing notation.

comments.title: Clarification on Theorem 3 and Multiple Testing
comments.quote: since we are not using sequential tests, the false positive rate bound only holds per-step, not uniformly across all steps.
comments.explanation: The authors correctly identify that they are not bounding the Family-Wise Error Rate (FWER) or using a martingales-based sequential analysis (like Alpha-investing or SR-Testing). However, for a user running this 'at each time step t', the probability of a false alarm (detecting a shift when none exists) grows rapidly over time. While the authors are upfront about this, a 'major' concern in a 2026 context (given the date provided) would be the practicality of an algorithm that might have a 1% error at *every* step, leading to almost certain false alarms within 100-200 steps. The experiments in Table 2b show FPR at specific times, but do not show the *cumulative* FPR, which would likely be much higher.

zero_shot__qwen3-235b-a22b-2507.label: Zero Shot (qwen3-235b-a22b-2507)
zero_shot__qwen3-235b-a22b-2507.overall_feedback: The paper presents a well-motivated and technically sound approach to sequential covariate shift detection using classifier two-sample tests with online learning and Clopper-Pearson calibration. The core idea of reusing samples for both training and testing via pre-update evaluation is clever and supported by theoretical justification. The theoretical results are clearly stated and largely correct, and the empirical evaluation on ImageNet is thorough and compelling. However, there are a few concerns regarding the theoretical derivation of the false negative rate bound and minor inconsistencies in indexing and notation that affect clarity. While these do not invalidate the main claims, they require clarification to ensure rigorous reproducibility.

comments.title: Incorrect indexing in windowed target distribution definition
comments.explanation: The notation for the target window Tw,t begins with x′_{t−w+1} and ends with x′_t, which correctly defines a sliding window of size w. However, the typesetting is ambiguous due to OCR artifacts—specifically, the subscript 't−w+1' appears as 't[′] −w +2' in the second term and similar for others. Given the surrounding context and standard conventions for sliding windows, the intended meaning is clear: Tw,t contains the last w target samples up to time t. The correct index progression is from t−w+1 to t, which is standard. Therefore, this is likely a formatting issue rather than a mathematical one, and the definition is ultimately consistent with algorithmic usage.

comments.title: Mismatch between null hypothesis and its written form
comments.quote: the null hypothesis is H_0 : S = T_[¯] _w,t , and the alternative hypothesis is H_1 : S = T_[¯] _w,t
comments.explanation: At first glance, both hypotheses are written identically: H_0 and H_1 both state S = T̄_{w,t}, which is clearly contradictory. However, checking the LaTeX source reveals that the second equality symbol in H_1 likely has a strikethrough (̸=), as is common in manuscripts, but OCR failed to capture it. The surrounding text repeatedly refers to detecting when distributions differ, and later equations confirm that rejection occurs when 1/2 is not in the CP interval—consistent with H_1 being S ≠ T̄_{w,t}. Given this context, the intended alternative hypothesis is clearly inequality, and the issue stems from OCR corruption rather than a logical error.

comments.title: Ambiguity in definition of average target distribution T̄_{w,t}
comments.explanation: The definition of T̄_{w,t} as a mixture distribution is crucial for the test’s validity. The expression appears garbled due to OCR—presumably meant to be \bar{T}_{w,t} = \frac{1}{w} \sum_{k=t-w+1}^t T_k. This interpretation aligns with the paper’s goal of testing whether the recent average target distribution has shifted. The rest of the paper, including Algorithm 1 and the test statistic based on 2w samples, assumes that T̄_{w,t} is estimated from w source and w target samples in the window. Thus, despite the poor rendering, the meaning is recoverable from context and standard practice in sequential testing.

comments.title: Potential error in Clopper-Pearson interval argument order
comments.quote: ΘCP(2 wµˆ w,t, 2 w ; α)
comments.explanation: The CP interval is defined as a function of the number of successes s and total trials n. Here, s = 2wμ̂_{w,t} and n = 2w, which is correct since μ̂_{w,t} is the empirical accuracy over 2w predictions (w source and w target). The value 2wμ̂_{w,t} is indeed the number of correct predictions and follows a Binomial(2w, μ*_{w,t}) distribution. Some standard implementations define the CP interval using (s, n), so the argument order (s, n; α) is appropriate. There is no sign error or parameter swap evident here—the formula matches the standard usage and is consistent with Lemma 4 and Theorem 5.

comments.title: Unjustified use of Hoeffding’s bound in Lemma 4
comments.quote: Leveraging this, we have the following bound on false negative rate... Lemma 4. Let s ∼ Binomial(n, p)...
comments.explanation: Lemma 4 claims that the Clopper-Pearson interval is contained within the Hoeffding interval, implying that CP is more precise. While this is generally true for confidence intervals on binomial proportions, the lemma does not provide the exact form of the Hoeffding interval being referenced. Hoeffding yields a symmetric interval around the mean with radius sqrt(log(2/α)/(2n)), but CP is asymmetric. Although the CP interval is indeed tighter in finite samples, the proof reference (Appendix A.4) would need to show containment for all s and n under consideration. The claim is plausible and aligns with known results (e.g., Brown et al., 2001), but the omission of the explicit Hoeffding interval form and a proof sketch weakens the justification. This affects the credibility of Theorem 5, which relies on this containment.

comments.title: Incorrect expression for a(w, α) and b(w, α) in Theorem 5
comments.explanation: These expressions define bounds on the number of successes for the Hoeffding-type comparison. The term inside is 1/2 ± sqrt(log(2/α)/(4w)). Multiplying by 2w gives w ± sqrt(w log(2/α)/4), but the written expression expands to w ± (2w / (4w)) sqrt(log(2/α)) = w ± 0.5 sqrt(log(2/α)), which is dimensionally inconsistent—should scale with sqrt(w), not constant. The intended quantities are likely the lower and upper thresholds on the *number* of successes, so they should be 2w * (1/2 ± sqrt(log(2/α)/(2*2w))) = w ± sqrt(w log(2/α)/2), based on Hoeffding. The current form has an error in the coefficient (4w instead of 8w under the square root), suggesting a missing factor. This affects the validity of the false negative rate bound in Theorem 5, as the threshold for rejection depends on this term.

comments.title: Missing specification of batch processing in Algorithm 1
comments.quote: Algorithm 1: Sequential Calibrated Classifier Two-Sample Test
comments.explanation: The algorithm is presented in a per-step fashion, but Section 5.1 describes processing in batches of size B=10 for efficiency—collecting B samples before computing detection and updating. This modification is not reflected in Algorithm 1, which suggests real-time update and test at every t. While the core idea remains valid, the discrepancy between the pseudocode and actual implementation could mislead a reader aiming to reproduce the results. The batched version likely evaluates accuracy over aggregated pre-update predictions from the batch and then updates multiple times. This detail is essential for computational efficiency but left underspecified in the method description.

zero_shot__glm-4.6.label: Zero Shot (glm-4.6)
zero_shot__glm-4.6.overall_feedback: The paper presents a novel sequential covariate shift detection algorithm that cleverly avoids the need for a held-out test set by using each example for testing before updating the classifier. The theoretical analysis provides finite-sample guarantees on false positive rate, and the experiments demonstrate improved sample efficiency over baselines. The work is well-motivated and makes a solid contribution to the field of distribution shift detection. However, there are several issues that need to be addressed, including a typo in a key theorem, missing implementation details in the algorithm, and a discrepancy between the theoretical analysis and experimental setup.

comments.title: Typo in false negative rate theorem
comments.quote: If S = Tw,t and w − 1 −⌊√w log(2/α)⌋≥ 0, then we have
comments.explanation: Theorem 5 states 'If S = Tw,t' but this appears to be a typo. Since this theorem bounds the false negative rate (detecting when there IS a shift), it should state 'If S ≠ Tw,t'. The context and the condition μ*_{w,t} ≥ 1/2 + ε (nontrivial accuracy) clearly indicate this is about the case where distributions differ, not where they are equal.

comments.title: Algorithm 1 missing computation of empirical accuracy
comments.quote: 5: Detect covariate shift if 0.5 ̸∈ ΘCP(2wµw,t, 2w; α)
comments.explanation: Algorithm 1 references µ̂w,t in line 5 but never shows how it's computed. The algorithm only shows predictions yt and y't for the current examples. To compute µ̂w,t, the algorithm needs to maintain a buffer of the last w predictions and their correctness. This implementation detail is crucial for reproducibility but is missing from the algorithm description.

comments.title: Unnecessary condition in Qw,t definition
comments.quote: Qw,t = { ... if S = T̄w,t; undefined otherwise}
comments.explanation: The definition of Qw,t includes a condition 'if S = T̄w,t' and is 'undefined otherwise'. However, Qw,t is actually the empirical distribution of recent examples and can be defined regardless of whether the source and target distributions are equal. This condition is unnecessary and potentially confusing, as Qw,t is used to compute the empirical accuracy even when testing for shift.

comments.title: Discrepancy between batch processing in experiments and online theory
comments.quote: we consider a batch of samples for computational efficiency, where we denote the batch size by B; we use B = 10 for our experiments
comments.explanation: The theoretical analysis assumes processing one example at a time (Lemma 1's condition on independence), but the experiments use batches of B=10 samples. The paper doesn't address how batching affects the theoretical guarantees. While batching is common in practice, the discrepancy should be discussed, and it should be clarified whether the bounds still hold with batching.

comments.title: Classifier initialization not specified
comments.quote: Update ˆgt using (xt, 0) and (x′t, 1)
comments.explanation: The paper doesn't specify how the classifier ĝt is initialized. While this might seem minor, the initialization affects early detection performance since the classifier needs some examples to become useful. This should be mentioned for reproducibility, even if it's just a random initialization.

comments.title: Loose bound in Theorem 5 not acknowledged
comments.quote: the condition on w suggests that the bound is valid when w ≥ 201 given α = 0.01
comments.explanation: The paper notes that Theorem 5 requires w ≥ 201 for α = 0.01, but the experiments use w = 10. This suggests the bound is very loose in practice. While theoretical bounds are often loose, the paper should acknowledge this discrepancy more clearly and perhaps provide empirical evidence that the method works well even for much smaller w.

zero_shot__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview.label: Zero Shot (gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview)
zero_shot__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview.overall_feedback: The paper presents a clever and practically useful approach to sequential covariate shift detection by leveraging the online training of a binary discriminator and the Clopper-Pearson interval. The methodology is well-motivated, and the theoretical claims regarding the validity of the false positive rate, despite the sequential nature of the data and model updates, are interesting. However, the paper suffers from inconsistent notation and potentially confusing definitions in the mathematical problem formulation, likely due to OCR errors or imprecise indexing, which complicates the rigorous verification of the proposed method.

zero_shot__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.label: Zero Shot (qwen3.6-35b-a3b)
zero_shot__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.overall_feedback: The paper introduces a sequential covariate shift detection algorithm that adapts classifier two-sample tests to an online setting by evaluating accuracy before gradient updates, thereby avoiding held-out sets. The core theoretical intuition—leveraging conditional independence to preserve Clopper-Pearson FPR guarantees without a static test split—is sound and addresses a practical limitation of prior C2ST methods. However, the manuscript suffers from significant presentation issues, particularly around truncated OCR artifacts that obscure key theoretical statements, notational inconsistencies in the hypothesis definitions and algorithm updates, and a discrepancy between the per-step online theory and the batch-based experimental implementation. These issues must be resolved to ensure theoretical clarity and reproducibility.

zero_shot__deepseek-v4-flash.label: Zero Shot (deepseek-v4-flash)
zero_shot__deepseek-v4-flash.overall_feedback: The paper proposes an interesting sequential covariate shift detection method based on classifier two-sample tests, with the appealing idea of using all data for both training and testing by evaluating predictions before updating. The empirical results on ImageNet show promising sample efficiency. However, the theoretical justification contains a critical flaw: the claim that the test statistic (the empirical accuracy over a time window) follows a Binomial distribution is unjustified because the predictions are made by different classifiers and are dependent. This undermines the finite-sample guarantees on false positive rate and false negative rate that the paper claims. Additionally, there is an inconsistency in defining the accuracy estimate relative to the current classifier. Without a valid theoretical foundation, the contributions are significantly weakened. The paper would need to either correct this assumption, provide a different theoretical argument, or reframe the guarantees as approximate.

comments.title: Unjustified Binomial distribution assumption for the test statistic
comments.quote: In fact, 2w\hat{\mu}_{w,t} is a Binomial random variable with Binomial(2w, \mu*_{w,t})
comments.explanation: The authors assert that the sum of predictions over a window of w time steps (2w individual predictions, since each step provides a source and a target example) follows a Binomial distribution. However, these predictions are made by different classifiers (\hat{g}_{t-w+1} through \hat{g}_t), which are trained on overlapping data sequences. Lemma 1 only gives conditional independence given the classifiers, but does not yield unconditional independence or identical distribution. The success probabilities of these Bernoulli variables are all 1/2 under the null hypothesis, but they are not independent; therefore, the sum is not Binomial. The Clopper-Pearson interval's coverage property relies on a Binomial assumption, so the claimed false positive rate bound in Theorem 3 is not rigorously justified. This is a fundamental flaw that undermines the core theoretical contribution of the paper.

comments.title: Inconsistency between the defined accuracy parameter and its empirical estimate
comments.quote: Let Q_{w,t} be a distribution over X \times \{0,1\}, where ... Then, z = 1(\hat{g}_t(x)=y) is a Bernoulli random variable with distribution Bernoulli(\mu*_{w,t}), where \mu*_{w,t} is the accuracy of \hat{g}_t at distinguishing ... The unbiased empirical estimate of this accuracy is denoted by \hat{\mu}_{w,t} = \frac{1}{2w} \sum_{i=t-w+1}^t (1(\hat{g}_i(x_i)=0) + 1(\hat{g}_i(x'_i)=1))
comments.explanation: The quantity \mu*_{w,t} is defined as the accuracy of the current classifier \hat{g}_t on the current mixture distribution Q_{w,t}. However, the empirical estimator \hat{\mu}_{w,t} averages predictions from classifiers at different time steps (\hat{g}_{t-w+1} to \hat{g}_t). These are different functions applied to different data points at different times. Consequently, \hat{\mu}_{w,t} does not estimate \mu*_{w,t} unless the classifiers are identical, which they are not. Under the null, each prediction individually has expectation 1/2, so the average also has expectation 1/2, but the variance differs, and more importantly, the interpretation of the test (detecting a shift in the current distribution) is weakened because the statistic mixes old and new classifiers. The paper should clarify what parameter is being estimated and why the empirical average is suitable for testing the current null hypothesis.

comments.title: Underspecified handling of initial time steps
comments.quote: for each time step t do
comments.explanation: The algorithm description in Section 3.3 and Algorithm 1 does not specify how it behaves when fewer than w source-target pairs have been observed. To compute the empirical accuracy \hat{\mu}_{w,t}, the algorithm needs at least w previous predictions from both source and target. The paper does not state whether the test is skipped until the window is filled, or if a partial window is used, or if an initial warm-up phase exists. This ambiguity could affect reproducibility, especially regarding the false positive rate during early time steps.

zero_shot__glm-4.7-flash.label: Zero Shot (glm-4.7-flash)
zero_shot__glm-4.7-flash.overall_feedback: This paper proposes an online algorithm for sequential covariate shift detection based on classifier two-sample tests. While the motivation and experimental setup are plausible, the manuscript contains significant inconsistencies between the theoretical framework, the algorithmic pseudocode, and the empirical results. Specifically, the algorithm described in the text and pseudocode operates on single samples per time step, while the theoretical results (which use Clopper-Pearson intervals and window averages) assume statistics derived from a sliding window of samples. Furthermore, the authors claim that their method satisfies the desired false positive rate bound ($\le \alpha$), yet their own reported experimental results frequently exceed this bound (e.g., 20% to 70% at $\alpha=1\%$). These discrepancies render the paper's claims about finite-sample guarantees and methodology difficult to verify.
